trace.rationale: Historical data shows that in MLB games, the first inning has a run scored approximately 55% of the time, based on general patterns from pre-cutoff seasons. No specific team or pitcher adjustments are available, so this baseline is used.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 50-55% of games have a run in the first inning, with slight variation by teams and pitchers. No specific team or pitcher info provided, so baseline estimate.
Historical data shows roughly 50-55% chance of a run in the first inning across MLB games, with slight variation by teams and pitchers.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 55-60% of games have a run scored in the first inning, with no specific team or pitcher info to adjust from baseline.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 55% of games have a run scored in the first inning, with no specific team or pitcher info to adjust.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 65% of first innings have at least one run scored, with no specific evidence to deviate from this baseline.
Historical data shows that in MLB games, approximately 55% of first innings see at least one run scored, based on league-wide averages from recent seasons.
Historical data shows roughly 45-50% of first innings have no runs, but slight edge to scoring due to typical offensive trends and no specific pitcher/weather info.
Historical data shows roughly 45-50% of MLB first innings have no runs, but slight edge to scoring due to average run expectancy.
